title: The four COBOL divisions
question: What are the four divisions of a COBOL program and what is each responsible for?
answer: Every COBOL program is organized into four divisions in a fixed order: IDENTIFICATION, ENVIRONMENT, DATA, and PROCEDURE. IDENTIFICATION names the program, ENVIRONMENT describes the machine and links logical files to physical devices, DATA declares all variables and file layouts, and PROCEDURE holds the executable logic. Only IDENTIFICATION and PROCEDURE are strictly required in modern compilers, but the ordering is always mandatory.
explanation: The division structure enforces a clean separation between metadata, platform configuration, data description, and behavior. This rigid layout is one reason COBOL programs are highly readable decades later: you always know where to find the file definitions (DATA) versus the logic (PROCEDURE). ENVIRONMENT is where hardware and file dependencies are isolated, which historically made programs easier to port between mainframes.
codeExample:
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PAYROLL.

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT EMP-FILE ASSIGN TO 'EMP.DAT'.

DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 WS-COUNT PIC 9(4) VALUE ZERO.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
MAIN-PARA.
    DISPLAY 'HELLO COBOL'.
    STOP RUN.
bestPractices: Always keep the divisions in the required order
Use IDENTIFICATION comments to document author and purpose
commonMistakes: Placing sections in the wrong division
Assuming all four divisions are mandatory in every program
interviewTips: Name the divisions in order and state one responsibility for each

title: WORKING-STORAGE and level 77
question: What is the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION used for, and how does a level 77 item differ from a level 01 item?
answer: WORKING-STORAGE SECTION, inside the DATA DIVISION, declares variables that persist for the life of the program run and are not tied to any file record. A level 01 entry can be a group item that contains subordinate fields, while a level 77 entry is always an independent, standalone elementary item that cannot have subordinates. Both are top-level, but 77 explicitly signals a non-group scalar.
explanation: WORKING-STORAGE data is initialized once (via VALUE clauses) and retains its contents across paragraphs, unlike LINKAGE or file record areas. Historically level 77 was preferred for isolated counters and flags to make intent clear, but modern style often just uses 01 for everything. Understanding that 77 items can never be subdivided is the key distinction interviewers look for.
codeExample:
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
77  WS-TOTAL        PIC 9(6)V99 VALUE ZERO.
01  WS-EMPLOYEE.
    05  WS-EMP-ID    PIC 9(5).
    05  WS-EMP-NAME  PIC X(30).
bestPractices: Give WORKING-STORAGE fields VALUE clauses to make defaults explicit
Group related fields under a single 01 record
commonMistakes: Trying to subordinate fields under a 77 item
Assuming WORKING-STORAGE resets between paragraphs
interviewTips: Stress that 77 items are always elementary and standalone

title: PICTURE (PIC) clauses
question: What does a PICTURE (PIC) clause define, and what do the symbols 9, X, A, V, and S mean?
answer: A PICTURE clause describes the category, size, and format of an elementary item. `9` is a numeric digit, `X` is any alphanumeric character, `A` is an alphabetic character, `V` is an implied (assumed) decimal point that occupies no storage, and `S` denotes a signed number. Repetition counts appear in parentheses, so `PIC 9(4)` means four numeric digits.
explanation: The PIC clause is how COBOL fixes field width and data type at compile time, which is central to its record-oriented, fixed-length data model. The `V` is crucial: it tells the compiler where the decimal falls for arithmetic without storing an actual point character, so `PIC 9(3)V99` holds five digits with two after the implied point. `S` controls whether a sign is tracked, affecting comparisons and arithmetic results.
codeExample:
01  WS-PRICE     PIC 9(3)V99  VALUE 125.50.
01  WS-NAME      PIC X(20).
01  WS-INITIAL   PIC A.
01  WS-BALANCE   PIC S9(7)V99 VALUE -100.00.
01  WS-COUNTER   PIC 9(4)     VALUE ZERO.
bestPractices: Use V for implied decimals in stored numbers, not a literal point
Add S when a field can legitimately hold negatives
commonMistakes: Confusing V (implied point) with an actual decimal character
Forgetting that X allows digits and letters but 9 does not allow letters
interviewTips: Explain that V and S occupy no character positions of their own

title: Level numbers, group vs elementary
question: How do level numbers create hierarchy in COBOL, and what is the difference between a group item and an elementary item?
answer: Level numbers (01 through 49) build a tree of data: a lower number is a parent and higher numbers nested beneath it are children. A group item has subordinate items and no PICTURE clause of its own; its value is the concatenation of its children. An elementary item has a PICTURE clause and no subordinates, holding an actual value. Referencing a group moves or compares all its bytes together.
explanation: This hierarchy lets a single record be treated both as a whole and as individual fields. Common convention uses increments of 5 (01, 05, 10, 15) so new levels can be inserted later without renumbering. When you MOVE to a group item, COBOL treats it as one alphanumeric field, which is why moving into a group can bypass individual field editing rules.
codeExample:
01  WS-DATE.
    05  WS-YEAR   PIC 9(4).
    05  WS-MONTH  PIC 9(2).
    05  WS-DAY    PIC 9(2).

*> WS-DATE is a group (8 chars); WS-YEAR is elementary.
MOVE '20260703' TO WS-DATE.
DISPLAY WS-MONTH.  *> shows 07
bestPractices: Number levels in steps of 5 to allow future insertions
Keep PICTURE clauses only on elementary items
commonMistakes: Adding a PIC clause to a group item
Assuming a group item performs numeric edits on a MOVE
interviewTips: Define group vs elementary by the presence of subordinates and a PIC clause

title: Condition names with level 88
question: What is a level 88 condition name and why is it useful?
answer: A level 88 entry defines a condition name: a readable Boolean tied to specific value(s) of its parent data item. Instead of comparing a field to raw literals, you test the condition name, and you can also SET it to make its parent take the associated value. This makes PROCEDURE logic self-documenting and centralizes the meaning of magic values.
explanation: Condition names attach directly beneath the elementary item they describe. Testing `IF END-OF-FILE` is clearer and less error-prone than `IF WS-EOF-FLAG = 'Y'`, and if the underlying code changes you only edit the 88 definition. `SET END-OF-FILE TO TRUE` assigns the first VALUE listed, giving symmetric, intent-revealing reads and writes.
codeExample:
01  WS-EOF-FLAG   PIC X VALUE 'N'.
    88  END-OF-FILE   VALUE 'Y'.
    88  MORE-RECORDS  VALUE 'N'.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
        READ EMP-FILE
            AT END SET END-OF-FILE TO TRUE
        END-READ
    END-PERFORM.
bestPractices: Use 88 levels for flags and status codes to improve readability
Use SET condition-name TO TRUE instead of moving literals
commonMistakes: Giving an 88 entry a PICTURE clause
Testing raw literals when a condition name already exists
interviewTips: Mention both testing an 88 and SET ... TO TRUE to show full understanding

title: PERFORM variations
question: Describe the main forms of the PERFORM statement in COBOL.
answer: PERFORM has several forms: `PERFORM paragraph-name` runs a paragraph once and returns; `PERFORM ... THRU ...` runs a range of paragraphs; `PERFORM n TIMES` repeats a fixed number of times; `PERFORM UNTIL condition` loops until the condition becomes true; and inline `PERFORM ... END-PERFORM` executes a block without a separate paragraph. `PERFORM VARYING` adds a loop counter for iterating.
explanation: PERFORM is COBOL's structured control mechanism, replacing unstructured GO TO branching. `PERFORM UNTIL` is tested before each iteration by default (WITH TEST BEFORE), so the body may run zero times. Inline PERFORM keeps small loops local and readable, while paragraph-based PERFORM promotes reuse and top-down decomposition of the PROCEDURE DIVISION.
codeExample:
PERFORM 100-INIT.

PERFORM 200-PROCESS 5 TIMES.

PERFORM VARYING WS-I FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL WS-I > 10
    ADD WS-I TO WS-SUM
END-PERFORM.

PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
    PERFORM 300-READ-NEXT
END-PERFORM.
bestPractices: Prefer PERFORM over GO TO for structured flow
Use inline PERFORM for short, local loops
commonMistakes: Expecting PERFORM UNTIL to always run at least once
Overusing PERFORM THRU with fragile paragraph ranges
interviewTips: List TIMES, UNTIL, VARYING, THRU, and inline forms to show breadth

title: Paragraphs vs sections
question: What is the difference between a paragraph and a section in the PROCEDURE DIVISION?
answer: A paragraph is a named block of one or more sentences and is the smallest unit you can PERFORM. A section is a named group of one or more paragraphs; performing a section runs all its paragraphs in order. Sections give a higher level of grouping and were historically important for overlay segmentation, while paragraphs are the everyday unit of modular logic.
explanation: Both are named entry points for PERFORM, but sections add an extra organizational layer and change some scoping rules (for example, PERFORM of a section runs through all contained paragraphs). Many shops standardize on paragraph-only structuring for simplicity, using numeric prefixes like 100-, 200- to imply ordering, and reserve sections for logical modules or when the compiler/coding standard requires them.
codeExample:
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
1000-MAIN SECTION.
1000-START.
    PERFORM 2000-READ.
    PERFORM 3000-PRINT.
    STOP RUN.

2000-READ.
    READ IN-FILE AT END SET END-OF-FILE TO TRUE END-READ.

3000-PRINT.
    DISPLAY WS-LINE.
bestPractices: Use consistent numeric prefixes to convey execution order
Keep paragraphs small and single-purpose
commonMistakes: Mixing section and paragraph structuring inconsistently
Forgetting that PERFORM of a section runs every paragraph in it
interviewTips: State that a section contains paragraphs and both are PERFORM targets

title: MOVE and data movement
question: How does the MOVE statement work, and what happens on truncation or type mismatch?
answer: MOVE copies data from a source into one or more receiving fields, converting and aligning according to the receiver's PICTURE. Numeric moves align on the decimal point and pad or truncate digits; alphanumeric moves align left and pad with spaces or truncate on the right. If the receiver is too small, high-order numeric digits or right-hand characters are silently lost.
explanation: MOVE is the workhorse of data transfer, and its behavior depends entirely on the categories of sender and receiver. Numeric-to-numeric respects the implied decimal and sign; alphanumeric-to-alphanumeric is a byte copy with space fill. Because truncation is silent, sizing receivers correctly is a frequent source of production bugs, which is why editing PIC clauses and careful field design matter.
codeExample:
01  WS-A  PIC 9(4)   VALUE 1234.
01  WS-B  PIC 9(2).
01  WS-C  PIC X(5)   VALUE 'HELLO'.
01  WS-D  PIC X(3).

MOVE WS-A TO WS-B.   *> WS-B = 34 (high digits lost)
MOVE WS-C TO WS-D.   *> WS-D = 'HEL' (right chars lost)
bestPractices: Size receiving fields to avoid unintended truncation
Use MOVE CORRESPONDING carefully and only with matching subfields
commonMistakes: Assuming MOVE reports an error on truncation
Confusing numeric decimal alignment with alphanumeric left alignment
interviewTips: Explain numeric vs alphanumeric alignment and silent truncation

title: COMPUTE vs arithmetic verbs
question: When would you use COMPUTE versus the individual arithmetic verbs (ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, DIVIDE)?
answer: COMPUTE evaluates a full arithmetic expression with operators and precedence into a result field, which is concise for multi-step formulas. The individual verbs (ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, DIVIDE) perform one operation in verbose English-like syntax and offer clauses like GIVING, REMAINDER, and ROUNDED. Use COMPUTE for readable formulas and the discrete verbs for simple single operations or when you need REMAINDER.
explanation: COMPUTE follows standard operator precedence (** then * and /, then + and -) and supports ROUNDED and ON SIZE ERROR. The discrete verbs can be clearer for a single accumulation and expose features COMPUTE lacks, such as DIVIDE ... GIVING ... REMAINDER. Intermediate result precision differs between styles, so choose consistently and always handle SIZE ERROR for overflow-prone calculations.
codeExample:
COMPUTE WS-NET ROUNDED = WS-GROSS - (WS-GROSS * WS-TAX-RATE)
    ON SIZE ERROR DISPLAY 'OVERFLOW'
END-COMPUTE.

ADD WS-QTY TO WS-TOTAL.
DIVIDE WS-TOTAL BY WS-COUNT
    GIVING WS-AVG REMAINDER WS-REM.
bestPractices: Use ROUNDED and ON SIZE ERROR for financial calculations
Prefer COMPUTE for multi-operator formulas to reduce temporaries
commonMistakes: Ignoring ON SIZE ERROR and getting silent truncated results
Assuming intermediate precision is identical across styles
interviewTips: Mention REMAINDER and ROUNDED as reasons to pick a specific style

title: Numeric editing PICTURE clauses
question: What are editing PICTURE symbols like Z, comma, decimal point, and $ used for?
answer: Editing PIC symbols format numeric data for display or reports. `Z` suppresses leading zeros (replacing them with spaces), the comma and period insert grouping and decimal punctuation, `$` and `*` add currency or check protection, and `-`/`CR`/`DB` show signs. Moving a numeric value into an edited field produces a human-readable, right-justified string; you cannot do arithmetic on an edited field.
explanation: Edited (report) items turn raw internal numbers into presentation form during the MOVE. For example `PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99` blanks leading zeros, inserts a thousands comma and an actual decimal point, and guarantees at least one digit before the point. Because the result is alphanumeric-like, edited fields are receiving-only for formatting and must not be used as arithmetic operands.
codeExample:
01  WS-AMT       PIC 9(5)V99  VALUE 01234.50.
01  WS-AMT-EDIT  PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99.
01  WS-PAY-EDIT  PIC $$$,$$9.99CR.

MOVE WS-AMT TO WS-AMT-EDIT.
*> WS-AMT-EDIT displays ' 1,234.50'
DISPLAY WS-AMT-EDIT.
bestPractices: Use edited fields only for output, never as arithmetic operands
Choose Z vs * based on blank vs asterisk zero suppression needs
commonMistakes: Trying to perform arithmetic on an edited field
Confusing the implied V with the inserted actual decimal point
interviewTips: Explain that editing happens during MOVE into the edited receiver

title: REDEFINES clause
question: What does the REDEFINES clause do and when is it appropriate to use?
answer: REDEFINES lets two or more data descriptions share the same storage area, giving alternative views of the same bytes. The redefining item does not allocate new memory; it overlays the memory of the item it redefines. It is useful for interpreting one buffer in multiple layouts, saving storage, or reusing a record area for different record types.
explanation: Because both descriptions map to identical bytes, changing one view changes the other. This is powerful for variant records (for example, a header field indicates which layout applies) and for reinterpreting data such as viewing an alphanumeric field as numeric. The redefining entry must have the same level number and generally must not include a VALUE clause; misuse can produce garbage when the actual bytes do not fit the alternate PICTURE.
codeExample:
01  WS-RECORD.
    05  WS-RAW      PIC X(10).
    05  WS-PARTS REDEFINES WS-RAW.
        10  WS-CODE  PIC X(2).
        10  WS-DESC  PIC X(8).

MOVE 'AB12345678' TO WS-RAW.
DISPLAY WS-CODE.  *> shows 'AB'
bestPractices: Use REDEFINES for variant records and memory reuse
Ensure the alternate PICTURE genuinely matches the stored bytes
commonMistakes: Putting a VALUE clause on a REDEFINES entry
Assuming REDEFINES allocates separate storage
interviewTips: Stress that REDEFINES overlays the same bytes, not new memory

title: OCCURS, tables, and subscripts
question: How do you define and access a table (array) in COBOL using OCCURS?
answer: The OCCURS clause turns a data item into a table by repeating it a fixed (or variable) number of times. You access elements with a subscript in parentheses, where subscripts are 1-based. OCCURS can be nested for multi-dimensional tables, and INDEXED BY defines an index for use with SEARCH. Variable-length tables use OCCURS DEPENDING ON tied to a count field.
explanation: COBOL tables are the equivalent of arrays but always start at index 1, not 0. A subscript is an ordinary numeric variable, whereas an index (INDEXED BY) is a special optimized displacement used by SET and SEARCH. OCCURS DEPENDING ON lets a table's length vary at runtime based on another field, which is common in variable-length records. Out-of-range subscripts are a classic cause of data corruption.
codeExample:
01  WS-SALES.
    05  WS-MONTH-AMT OCCURS 12 TIMES
                     INDEXED BY WS-IDX
                     PIC 9(6)V99.

MOVE 1500.00 TO WS-MONTH-AMT (3).
PERFORM VARYING WS-IDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL WS-IDX > 12
    ADD WS-MONTH-AMT (WS-IDX) TO WS-TOTAL
END-PERFORM.
bestPractices: Remember subscripts are 1-based, not 0-based
Use INDEXED BY with SEARCH for efficient lookups
commonMistakes: Using a 0 or out-of-range subscript
Confusing a numeric subscript with an INDEXED BY index
interviewTips: Contrast subscripts and indexes, and mention OCCURS DEPENDING ON

title: COPY statement and copybooks
question: What is a copybook and how does the COPY statement help with code reuse?
answer: A copybook is a reusable fragment of COBOL source, typically a data structure such as a record layout, stored in a separate library member. The COPY statement textually includes that member at compile time, so many programs share one authoritative definition. The REPLACING option can substitute text (like prefixes) during the copy to adapt a generic layout.
explanation: Copybooks are how mainframe shops keep record definitions consistent across dozens of programs: change the copybook once, recompile dependents, and every program agrees on the layout. Because COPY is a compile-time textual expansion, it does not add runtime overhead. REPLACING allows one copybook to be reused with different field-name prefixes, avoiding name clashes when a program includes two similar structures.
codeExample:
*> In program:
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
COPY EMPREC.

COPY EMPREC REPLACING ==PREFIX== BY ==WS==.

*> Copybook EMPREC contains:
*> 01  PREFIX-EMPLOYEE.
*>     05  PREFIX-ID    PIC 9(5).
*>     05  PREFIX-NAME  PIC X(30).
bestPractices: Centralize shared record layouts in copybooks
Use COPY REPLACING to reuse a layout with different prefixes
commonMistakes: Duplicating record layouts instead of using a copybook
Forgetting to recompile all programs after a copybook change
interviewTips: Describe COPY as a compile-time textual include, not a runtime call

title: EVALUATE statement
question: How does the EVALUATE statement work and why is it preferred over nested IFs?
answer: EVALUATE is COBOL's case/switch construct: it compares one or more subjects against WHEN branches and executes the first that matches, with an optional WHEN OTHER default. It supports value matching, ranges (THRU), conditions (WHEN condition), and multiple subjects evaluated together (WHEN ... ALSO ...). It replaces deeply nested IF/ELSE chains with flat, readable decision logic.
explanation: EVALUATE TRUE lets each WHEN carry its own arbitrary condition, effectively a clean if-elseif ladder. Multi-subject EVALUATE with ALSO forms a decision table, matching combinations across columns, which is far clearer than nested IFs. Using WHEN OTHER guards against unhandled cases, improving robustness and making the intended branches explicit for maintainers.
codeExample:
EVALUATE WS-GRADE
    WHEN 'A' DISPLAY 'EXCELLENT'
    WHEN 'B' THRU 'C' DISPLAY 'PASS'
    WHEN OTHER DISPLAY 'FAIL'
END-EVALUATE.

EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN WS-AGE < 13  MOVE 'CHILD' TO WS-CAT
    WHEN WS-AGE < 20  MOVE 'TEEN'  TO WS-CAT
    WHEN OTHER        MOVE 'ADULT' TO WS-CAT
END-EVALUATE.
bestPractices: Always include WHEN OTHER as a safety default
Use EVALUATE TRUE for condition ladders instead of nested IFs
commonMistakes: Omitting WHEN OTHER and silently ignoring unmatched cases
Forgetting END-EVALUATE and mis-scoping following statements
interviewTips: Mention multi-subject EVALUATE with ALSO as a decision table

title: File handling: SELECT, FD, READ, WRITE
question: Walk through the pieces needed to process a file in COBOL: SELECT, FD, OPEN, READ, WRITE, CLOSE.
answer: You declare a logical file with SELECT ... ASSIGN in the ENVIRONMENT DIVISION's FILE-CONTROL, describe its record layout with an FD entry in the DATA DIVISION's FILE SECTION, then in the PROCEDURE DIVISION OPEN it (INPUT/OUTPUT/I-O/EXTEND), READ or WRITE records, and CLOSE it. SELECT links the program's file name to the physical dataset, and FD defines the record structure.
explanation: This separation is deliberate: SELECT isolates the physical/environment binding, FD defines the data shape, and the PROCEDURE verbs perform I/O. OPEN mode must match intent (INPUT for reading, EXTEND for appending). READ ... AT END detects end-of-file, and FILE STATUS codes report I/O success or errors. Always CLOSE files to flush buffers and release resources.
codeExample:
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT EMP-FILE ASSIGN TO 'EMP.DAT'
        FILE STATUS IS WS-STATUS.

DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD  EMP-FILE.
01  EMP-RECORD    PIC X(80).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    OPEN INPUT EMP-FILE.
    READ EMP-FILE AT END SET END-OF-FILE TO TRUE END-READ.
    CLOSE EMP-FILE.
bestPractices: Check FILE STATUS after each I/O operation
Match OPEN mode to the intended access (INPUT/OUTPUT/I-O/EXTEND)
commonMistakes: Reading without an AT END clause
Forgetting to CLOSE files and losing buffered writes
interviewTips: Trace the flow SELECT -> FD -> OPEN -> READ/WRITE -> CLOSE

title: Sequential vs indexed files
question: What is the difference between sequential and indexed (VSAM KSDS) file organizations in COBOL?
answer: Sequential files store records one after another and are read strictly in order, ideal for batch processing of every record. Indexed files (typically VSAM KSDS) maintain a key so records can be retrieved directly by key value as well as read sequentially in key order. You choose the organization with ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL or INDEXED and, for indexed, specify RECORD KEY.
explanation: Indexed organization adds an index structure keyed on a RECORD KEY, enabling random READ by key, START to position, and dynamic access mixing random and sequential reads. This is far faster for keyed lookups than scanning a sequential file, at the cost of extra storage and maintenance of the index. Sequential remains optimal for full-file passes where every record is processed anyway.
codeExample:
SELECT CUST-FILE ASSIGN TO 'CUST.KSDS'
    ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
    ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC
    RECORD KEY IS CUST-ID
    FILE STATUS IS WS-STATUS.

*> Random read by key:
MOVE 1005 TO CUST-ID.
READ CUST-FILE
    INVALID KEY DISPLAY 'NOT FOUND'
END-READ.
bestPractices: Use indexed files when random key access is needed
Use ACCESS MODE DYNAMIC to mix random and sequential reads
commonMistakes: Using sequential organization for frequent keyed lookups
Omitting the INVALID KEY clause on keyed reads and writes
interviewTips: Explain RECORD KEY and the random vs sequential access trade-off

title: GO TO vs structured programming
question: Why is GO TO discouraged in modern COBOL and what should you use instead?
answer: GO TO transfers control unconditionally to another paragraph, and heavy use creates tangled 'spaghetti' logic that is hard to follow and maintain. Structured programming favors PERFORM, IF/EVALUATE, and inline loops, which keep control flow localized and return to the caller predictably. Modern standards restrict GO TO, sometimes allowing only GO TO ... within a PERFORM THRU range for early exit.
explanation: PERFORM gives you call-and-return semantics so logic is composed of reusable, self-contained paragraphs, unlike GO TO which jumps and never returns. The historically tolerated pattern is a controlled GO TO to an exit paragraph inside a PERFORM THRU range. Eliminating arbitrary GO TOs improves readability, testability, and reduces defects in long-lived programs.
codeExample:
*> Discouraged:
*>    IF WS-FLAG = 'X' GO TO 900-ERROR.

*> Structured alternative:
PERFORM 100-VALIDATE THRU 100-EXIT.

100-VALIDATE.
    IF WS-FLAG = 'X'
        MOVE 'BAD' TO WS-RESULT
        GO TO 100-EXIT
    END-IF.
    MOVE 'OK' TO WS-RESULT.
100-EXIT.
    EXIT.
bestPractices: Prefer PERFORM, EVALUATE, and inline loops over GO TO
If GO TO is unavoidable, confine it to a PERFORM THRU exit
commonMistakes: Using GO TO to jump between unrelated paragraphs
Creating fall-through logic that is hard to trace
interviewTips: Frame the answer around maintainability and PERFORM call/return

title: Packed decimal (COMP-3)
question: What is COMP-3 (packed decimal) and why is it commonly used on mainframes?
answer: COMP-3 stores numbers in packed decimal format, where each digit occupies half a byte (a nibble) and the final nibble holds the sign. This packs two digits per byte, roughly halving storage versus display (unpacked) numeric fields, and arithmetic on it is efficient on IBM mainframe hardware. It is the default choice for numeric fields in financial records.
explanation: A USAGE DISPLAY numeric field stores one digit per byte, while COMP-3 uses n/2 + 1 bytes for an n-digit number. Beyond space savings, packed decimal preserves exact decimal precision, which is essential for money where binary floating point would introduce rounding errors. The trailing sign nibble (C for positive, D for negative, F for unsigned) is why sizes are computed as (digits + 1) / 2 bytes.
codeExample:
01  WS-AMOUNT  PIC S9(7)V99  COMP-3.
*> 9 digits -> (9 + 1) / 2 = 5 bytes of storage

01  WS-DISPLAY PIC 9(9).
*> 9 digits -> 9 bytes of storage

COMPUTE WS-AMOUNT = WS-AMOUNT + 100.50.
bestPractices: Use COMP-3 for large numeric and monetary fields to save space
Keep a sign (S) on packed fields that can be negative
commonMistakes: Assuming COMP-3 stores one digit per byte
Displaying a COMP-3 field directly without moving to an edited field
interviewTips: State the storage formula (digits + 1) / 2 bytes and the sign nibble

title: STOP RUN vs GOBACK
question: What is the difference between STOP RUN, GOBACK, and EXIT PROGRAM?
answer: STOP RUN terminates the entire run unit and returns control to the operating system, regardless of how deep the call chain is. GOBACK returns control to the caller: to the OS if used in the main program, or to the calling program if used in a subprogram. EXIT PROGRAM returns from a called subprogram to its caller but has no effect in a main program.
explanation: The distinction matters in called-module architectures. Using STOP RUN inside a subprogram abruptly ends the whole run unit, which is usually wrong; GOBACK is the safe, context-aware choice that behaves correctly whether the module is a main program or a subprogram. EXIT PROGRAM is the traditional subprogram return but is a no-op at the top level, so many shops standardize on GOBACK everywhere.
codeExample:
*> Main program:
MAIN-PARA.
    CALL 'SUBPROG'.
    GOBACK.        *> returns to OS here

*> Subprogram SUBPROG:
SUB-PARA.
    DISPLAY 'IN SUB'.
    GOBACK.        *> returns to MAIN, not the OS
bestPractices: Prefer GOBACK for portable termination in both main and subprograms
Avoid STOP RUN inside called subprograms
commonMistakes: Using STOP RUN in a subprogram and killing the whole run unit
Expecting EXIT PROGRAM to terminate a main program
interviewTips: Explain behavior of each in main vs subprogram context

title: String handling: STRING, UNSTRING, INSPECT
question: How do STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT handle text manipulation in COBOL?
answer: STRING concatenates multiple source fields into one target, optionally using DELIMITED BY to stop at a delimiter or SIZE. UNSTRING splits one field into several targets using delimiters, and can report counts and remaining data. INSPECT examines a field to TALLYING (count occurrences) or REPLACING (substitute characters), useful for counting or cleaning data.
explanation: These verbs give COBOL practical text processing without pointer arithmetic. STRING with POINTER tracks the position as it builds output; UNSTRING with DELIMITED BY parses fields like CSV segments and can set a TALLYING count of extracted fields. INSPECT ... REPLACING ALL is the idiom for tasks like converting spaces to zeros or stripping characters, while INSPECT ... TALLYING counts matches for validation.
codeExample:
STRING WS-FIRST DELIMITED BY SPACE
       ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
       WS-LAST  DELIMITED BY SPACE
    INTO WS-FULL-NAME
END-STRING.

UNSTRING WS-CSV DELIMITED BY ','
    INTO WS-A WS-B WS-C
END-UNSTRING.

INSPECT WS-TEXT REPLACING ALL SPACE BY '0'.
INSPECT WS-TEXT TALLYING WS-CNT FOR ALL 'A'.
bestPractices: Use DELIMITED BY SIZE to insert literal separators with STRING
Use INSPECT TALLYING for counting before validating data
commonMistakes: Overflowing the target and losing data without a POINTER/ON OVERFLOW check
Forgetting DELIMITED BY, causing unexpected padding
interviewTips: Match each verb to a task: build, split, and inspect/replace
